assertion_results.text: The answer recommends `@pytest.mark.parametrize` for the repeated input cases
assertion_results.evidence: Direct recommendation and usage: "Use pytest.mark.parametrize for a table-driven test." and in code: "@pytest.mark.parametrize("email, expected", CASES)"; also later: "@pytest.mark.parametrize("email", VALIDS)" and "@pytest.mark.parametrize("email, exc", INVALIDS)"

assertion_results.text: The answer shows or describes a table of input and expected values in one focused test
assertion_results.evidence: Provides a single focused test driven by a table of pairs: "CASES = [ pytest.param("simple@example.com", True, id="simple"), ... ]" and "@pytest.mark.parametrize("email, expected", CASES)\ndef test_is_valid_email(email, expected):\n    assert is_valid_email(email) is expected"

assertion_results.text: The answer avoids recommending many near-duplicate test functions for each case
assertion_results.evidence: States: "Drive one or two small test functions from a list of cases so each row is a separate subtest with clear IDs." and "Keep one parametrized test with (email, expected) rows." The examples use parametrization rather than separate near-duplicate tests per case.
